records.ety: [OF. trahiner, traner,F. traner, LL. trahinare, trainare, fr. L. trahere to draw. See Trail.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Trained (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Training.]

defs: To draw along; to trail; to drag.
quotes: In hollow cube Training his devilish enginery.

defs: To draw by persuasion, artifice, or the like; to attract by stratagem; to entice; to allure.
quotes: If but a dozen French Were there in arms, they would be as a call To train ten thousand English to their side.
O, train me not, sweet mermaid, with thy note.
This feast, I'll gage my life, Is but a plot to train you to your ruin.

defs: To teach and form by practice; to educate; to exercise; to discipline; as, to train the militia to the manual exercise; to train soldiers to the use of arms.
quotes: Our trained bands, which are the trustiest and most proper strength of a free nation.
The warrior horse here bred he's taught to train.

defs: To break, tame, and accustom to draw, as oxen.
To lead or direct, and form to a wall or espalier; to form to a proper shape, by bending, lopping, or pruning; as, to train young trees.
quotes: He trained the young branches to the right hand or to the left.

defs: To trace, as a lode or any mineral appearance, to its head.
quotes: Train up a child in the way he should go; and when he is old, he will not depart from it.
The first Christians were, by great hardships, trained up for glory.

defs: To be drilled in military exercises; to do duty in a military company.
To prepare by exercise, diet, instruction, etc., for any physical contest; as, to train for a boat race.

records.ety: [F. train, OF. tran, trahin; cf. (for some of the senses) F. traine. See Train, v.]
usage: -- Train, Cars. At one time “train” meaning railroad train was also referred to in the U. S. by the phrase “the cars”. In the 1913 dictionary the usage was described thus: “Train is the word universally used in England with reference to railroad traveling; as, I came in the morning train. In the United States, the phrase the cars has been extensively introduced in the room of train; as, the cars are late; I came in the cars. The English expression is obviously more appropriate, and is prevailing more and more among Americans, to the exclusion of the cars.”

defs: That which draws along; especially, persuasion, artifice, or enticement; allurement.
Hence, something tied to a lure to entice a hawk; also, a trap for an animal; a snare.
quotes: With cunning trains him to entrap un wares.

defs: That which is drawn along in the rear of, or after, something; that which is in the hinder part or rear.
That part of a gown which trails behind the wearer.
The after part of a gun carriage; the trail.
The tail of a bird.
A number of followers; a body of attendants; a retinue; a suite.
quotes: The king's daughter with a lovely train.
My train are men of choice and rarest parts.

defs: A consecution or succession of connected things; a series.
quotes: The train of ills our love would draw behind it.
Rivers now Stream and perpetual draw their humid train.
Other truths require a train of ideas placed in order.

defs: Regular method; process; course; order; as, things now in a train for settlement.
quotes: If things were once in this train, . . . our duty would take root in our nature.

defs: The number of beats of a watch in any certain time.
A line of gunpowder laid to lead fire to a charge, mine, or the like.
A connected line of cars or carriages on a railroad; -- called also railroad train.
A heavy, long sleigh used in Canada for the transportation of merchandise, wood, and the like.
A roll train; as, a 12-inch train.
The aggregation of men, animals, and vehicles which accompany an army or one of its subdivisions, and transport its baggage, ammunition, supplies, and reserve materials of all kinds.
